sample_tweets.text: 🚨 BREAKING:  🇰🇵 North Korea: "Any cyberattack by the US-Israel alliance against Iran's cyber infrastructure will be met with a comprehensive cyber and military counter-response that would paralyze Washington's cyber network."  🇮🇱 Israel: "We do not wish to escalate tensions, but we are fully prepared to neutralize any threat, whether it comes from Tehran or from a desperate government in Pyongyang."

An Anthropic executive just said China’s open-source AI models could hand cyberattack capability to anyone with a download button.  Not next year. 12 months out.  He called it “mythos class” cyber capability - the kind normally locked behind frontier labs. His words: “I don’t think there’s anything we can do to stop it.”  The logic is simple. Intelligence beats everything. Cheaper, weaker models don’t win users. Frontier models do. So the lagging models get open-sourced, and the danger sits in the gap between “weaker” and “still dangerous.”  He worked in China for a year. Speech recognition, mostly.  One detail stuck with him: a China team told him, flatly, “we don’t care about privacy in China.” That’s how they got the training data.  His real fear isn’t the tech. It’s the state behind it.  He pointed to the Uyghurs. To Hong Kong critics silenced through networks reaching straight into US companies.  “An authoritarian state and high-tech authoritarian state” - his words for what happens when the CCP’s playbook fuses with AI.  His comparison: 1984. Or worse.  Then the pivot: AI isn’t fated to end there. It could go pro-democracy. Equal justice, delivered at scale. Or the other direction entirely.  He said the outcome depends on 3 actors: AI companies, governments, and the rest of us. “I see us as having a responsibility here.”  No hedge. No disclaimer. Just a countdown he says nobody can stop.

3| The analysis defines six macroeconomic stress scenarios for the EUDI ecosystem, based on historical events and IMF/ECB projections. Each scenario is assessed by three impacts: Eurozone GDP change, EUDI adoption rate change, and service provider ROI change. The most adverse scenario is a prolonged pandemic, simulating a 5.2% GDP drop, 15% lower adoption, and 11% lower ROI. This is driven by prioritizing health spending over digital investments, reduced physical interactions for biometric enrollment, and increased telework raising digital identity demand but delaying deployment budgets. Conversely, a focused cyberattack scenario, with a moderate 2.1% GDP impact, causes the steepest ROI decline at 15%, due to immediate user trust loss and high remediation costs from an identity wallet security breach.

The Detection Gap: MITRE ATT&CK T1140 and T1105 | MITRE ATT&CK Walkthrough: T1140 (Deobfuscate/Decod

sample_tweets.text: Los actores APT cada vez dependen menos de infraestructura propia para sus servidores de Command and Control (C2). En su lugar, abusan de plataformas legítimas como Telegram, utilizando su API o bots para establecer canales de comunicación cifrados que se mezclan con el tráfico normal y pueden evadir controles tradicionales de firewall y listas de reputación.  Los enlaces hacia el C2 pueden ocultarse en la descripción de canales, grupos o perfiles, e incluso dentro de conversaciones de bots privados, dificultando su descubrimiento durante tareas de OSINT o análisis de infraestructura. En escenarios más sofisticados, el primer enlace consultado por el malware puede apuntar a un grupo público aparentemente legítimo, utilizado únicamente como punto intermedio (dead drop resolver). Desde ese grupo, el malware obtiene un segundo enlace alojado en la descripción, un mensaje fijado o una conversación del bot, donde se encuentra la ubicación real del C2. Como resultado, las primeras conexiones observadas durante el análisis apuntan a un grupo completamente ajeno al atacante, dejando como rastro un recurso aparentemente inocente y desviando la atención de la infraestructura real.  El objetivo no es que Telegram aloje el malware, sino utilizarlo como puente de comunicación entre el implante y el operador, aprovechando una plataforma ampliamente permitida en entornos corporativos. Esta técnica está alineada con MITRE ATT&CK T1102.002 – Bidirectional Communication, donde se utilizan servicios web legítimos para el intercambio de comandos y la exfiltración de información.  ic3.gov/CSA/2026/260320.pdf

Encontramos os seguintes comandos no DC01 ( Imagem 1 ) e vou explicar o que cada um deles significa  - > lsadump::dcsync /domain:northwind.local /user:krbtgt :   Esse é um comando do mimikatz, o DCSync abusa do protocolo de replicação do Active Directory: o atacante finge ser outro Domain Controller e pede ao DC01 pra "replicar" as credenciais sem tocar no disco, sem dump de LSASS, pedir especificamente o usuário krbtgt é o objetivo mais valioso do AD: com o hash do krbtgt, ele forja Golden Tickets e vira o dono do domínio indefinidamente, mesmo depois de você "limpar" tudo.   MITRE ATT&CK: T1003.006 (DCSync)  -> vssadmin create shadow /for=C:  :   Cria uma Volume Shadow Copy (cópia-sombra) do disco C:. Por quê? O arquivo mais importante do DC, o NTDS.dit, o banco de dados que contém todas as contas e hashes do domínio,  fica travado pelo sistema enquanto o AD roda, você não consegue copiá-lo direto. A shadow copy é uma foto congelada do volume de onde dá pra extrair o arquivo travado. É o passo preparatório do próximo comando.   MITRE ATT&CK: T1006 / T1003.003  -> ntdsutil "ac i ntds" "ifm" "create full C:\Windows\Temp\ifm" q q  :   O ntdsutil é uma ferramenta legítima de administração do AD (mais um LOLBin), aqui abusada, o modo IFM (Install From Media) foi feito pra criar mídia de instalação de novos DCs, mas de quebra ele exporta uma cópia completa do NTDS.dit + a hive SYSTEM (que tem a chave pra descriptografar os hashes) pra C:\Windows\Temp\ifm Traduzindo: o atacante acabou de extrair o banco de credenciais inteiro do domínio  MITRE ATT&CK: T1003.003 (NTDS)  7z a -pInfected C:\Windows\Temp\ifm.7z C:\Windows\Temp\ifm  Usa o 7-Zip pra compactar a pasta ifm (com o NTDS.dit dentro) num arquivo .7z protegido por senha (-pInfected "Infected" é a senha, uma convenção comum em amostras de malware). Dois objetivos: reduzir o tamanho pra exfiltrar mais rápido e encriptar pra escapar de inspeção de conteúdo (DLP) na saída. MITRE: T1560.001 (Archive via Utility). Logo depois disso, na sua cadeia, vem a conexão de saída do DC01 pro IP do atacante na porta 443 a exfiltração de fato (T1041).

sample_tweets.text: #threatreport #LowCompleteness Threat Intelligence Report: The Pro-Iran Hacktivist Ecosystem 2026 | 09-07-2026 Source: dti.domaintools.com/research… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Ddos-for-hire Cyber_islamic_resistance Islamic_cyber_resistance (🧠motivation: propaganda) Fatimiyoun (🧠motivation: sabotage) Dark_storm_team (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Handala-hacking-team (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Keymous Dienet Russian_legion (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Killnet (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Cyber_fattah_team (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Rippersec (🧠motivation: hacktivism, propaganda) Cyb3r_drag0nz (🧠motivation: propaganda) Evil_markhors Nation_of_saviors (🧠motivation: propaganda) Hider_nex Noname057 (🧠motivation: hacktivism) Serverkillers  💀Threats: Credential_harvesting_technique, Password_spray_technique, Ddosia_botnet,  🎯Victims: Government, Telecommunications, Healthcare, Finance, Logistics, Open source infrastructure, Critical sectors, Israeli infrastructure, Western infrastructure, Public sector, ...  🏭Industry: Critical_infrastructure, Financial, Healthcare, Logistic, Government, Telco  🌐Geo: Russian, Iraq, Iranian, Israel, Middle east, Israeli, Afghan, Iran, United states, Kuwait, Malaysia  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 3 🛠️Technics: 0  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1078, T1190, T1485, T1491.002, T1498, T1588.002  💽Software: Telegram, Ubuntu  💻Platforms: cross-platform  #threatreport: The decentralized cyber ecosystem aligned with Iran, primarily operating in response to the U.S.-Iranian conflict, includes a diverse array of hacktivist groups, ideological militants, and influence networks. This loose coalition engages in activities such as DDoS attacks, website defacements, and hack-and-leak operations, notably facilitated through Telegram channels and dedicated websites. Attack claims emerge rapidly, providing actors plausible deniability and differentiating them from formal state entities while amplifying their impact during crises.  The technical capabilities displayed by these groups remain predominantly unsophisticated, with a focus on DDoS campaigns, propaganda, and extortion-style messaging rather than advanced intrusions. Their primary objective is not displayed capability but the ability to generate psychological and political pressure through coordinated efforts that leverage visibility and ideological narratives. Groups such as 313 Team exemplify this approach, performing high-profile DDoS attacks, such as targeted disruptions against Canonical and Ubuntu infrastructure, utilizing commercial DDoS-for-hire services rather than custom malware.  Other actors within the ecosystem include Handala Hack Team, known for its more sophisticated hack-and-leak strategies; Cyber Fattah Team, which engages in disruptive propaganda; and Fatimiyoun Cyber Team, focusing on critical infrastructure threats. Each group contributes to an overarching narrative of coalition-based cyber warfare, where even low-level operations can achieve significant media and psychological impact.  The threat landscape is characterized by a range of capabilities, from moderate to high risk for DDoS activities, disruption operations, and propaganda amplification. While some groups, like Dark Storm Team, blend traditional ideological objectives with criminal methodologies such as ransomware, others, like APT Iran, operate more as branding efforts without evident sophisticated operational skills.  Notably, groups such as Evil Markhors and Conquerors Electronic Army focus on credential harvesting and DDoS activities, contributing to the coalition's operational effectiveness by enhancing the potential for larger-scale attacks. Similarly, opportunistic actors like NoName057(16) and Killnet demonstrate increasing convergence of anti-Western narratives with the broader Iranian-aligned cyber environment, facilitating participation in coordinated disruption campaigns despite their varied ideologies.  In essence, the pro-Iran cyber ecosystem represents an evolution towards decentralized digital proxy warfare, prioritizing rapid disruption and psychological warfare over complex technical operations. Defensive strategies against this ecosystem center around enhancing DDoS readiness, employing web application firewalls, enforcing multi-factor authentication, and preparing for the reputational risks associated with inflated breach claims and real-time responses. Success hinges not just on thwarting intrusions but managing disruption and mitigating alert fatigue stemming from these coordinated, high-volume cyber activities.

Just in: Weekly preview: Wall Street’s Q 2 earnings season kicks off, TSMC and ASML test AI demand; U.S. CPI/PPI data for June arrives; Warsh’s congressional debut; U.S.-Iran “fight and talk” affects oil prices.  Overview of the new week’s important schedule Warsh attended the Congressional hearing for the first time as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, and the market focused on interest rate signals throughout the week. Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh will attend Congress's semi-annual monetary policy hearings for two consecutive days on Tuesday and Wednesday.  This is the first time that Warsh has gone to Congress as chairman of the Federal Reserve to testify on monetary policy since taking office, and it will also become the most important trading event of the week.  At 22:00 on Tuesday (this article is Beijing time), Warsh will attend a hearing of the House of Representatives Financial Services Committee; on Wednesday at 22:00, he will transfer to the Senate Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs Committee to testify. Both hearings focused on the Fed's semiannual monetary policy report to Congress.  The biggest highlight of this hearing is that the U.S. June CPI released at 20:30 on Tuesday is only 90 minutes away from the first hearing. Warsh can hardly avoid the issues of inflation and interest rate paths.  Markets will focus on how he evaluates the latest inflation data, whether the shock to energy prices is sustainable, and whether he will leave open the possibility of a rate hike in July or September.  The June FOMC statement deleted traditional forward guidance on interest rates, and Warsh later emphasized that the Fed will make judgments based on data and economic prospects. If CPI cools down and Warsh weakens his hawkish stance, U.S.  bond yields and the US dollar may face downward pressure, and technology growth stocks and gold are expected to gain support; if inflation exceeds expectations and Warsh does not rule out further tightening of policy, the market may continue to strengthen interest rate hike trades. In addition, many Fed officials will speak publicly.  At 00:30 on Tuesday, Fed Governor Waller gave a speech. At 01:00 on Wednesday, 2027 FOMC Voting Committee and Chicago Fed President Goolsby participated in the fireside chat; at 20:45, FOMC Permanent Voting Committee and New York Fed President Williams delivered a speech.  At 06:30 on Thursday, Mussallem, the 2028 FOMC voting committee member and President of the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, delivered a speech. At 00:30 on Friday, 2026 FOMC voting committee and Dallas Fed President Logan delivered a speech; at 07:00, Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Jefferson delivered a speech on economic and monetary policy. The U.S.  CPI and PPI in June were released one after another, and inflation and consumption data tested expectations for interest rate hikes. The U.S. June CPI and core CPI will be released at 20:30 on Tuesday. Many institutions predict that the overall U.S.  CPI year-on-year growth rate in June may drop to 3.8%-3.9% from 4.2% in May, and the core CPI year-on-year growth rate is expected to remain around 2.9%. Due to the slight difference in the forecast caliber of institutions, the market is really concerned about the month-on-month data and changes in housing, services, energy and other sub-items.  If core inflation falls significantly, the market may reduce its bets on raising interest rates within the year; conversely, if rising energy prices begin to spread to the prices of goods and services, expectations for further tightening of policy by the Federal Reserve may increase. At 20:30 on Wednesday, the U.S.  June PPI and core PPI will take over, providing investors with the latest clues to upstream price pressures. The U.S. June retail sales released at 20:30 on Thursday will test the impact of high interest rates and rising prices on household consumption.  For the market, next week's inflation data will not be traded separately, but will need to be judged in conjunction with "CPI-Wash Hearing-PPI-Retail Sales". Whether the data and Warsh's statements corroborate each other will determine the direction of U.S. bond yields, the U.S. dollar, gold and technology growth stocks.  The United States and Iran are "fighting and talking at the same time", and the situation in the Strait of Hormuz continues to dominate oil prices The situation between the United States and Iran will continue to be the biggest non-scheduled risk next week. The two sides launched military strikes against each other again this week.  Trump announced that the previously agreed ceasefire arrangement had ended, but at the same time said that the United States agreed to continue negotiations with Iran.  As of the weekend, the United States demanded that Iran publicly commit to stopping attacks on merchant ships and ensuring free and safe navigation in the Strait of Hormuz; the Iranian Foreign Minister went to Oman to discuss arrangements for navigation in the Strait with regional mediators.  While negotiations continue to advance, Iran once again announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and attacked US military bases in Gulf countries. The US military also launched a new round of attacks on Iranian targets. The situation showed obvious characteristics of "fighting and talking".  Next week, the market will need to focus on whether Iran accepts the navigation request proposed by the United States, whether Oman's mediation can form a temporary arrangement, and whether the United States and Iran will expand military operations again.  If stable navigation in the strait is restored, the geo-premium of crude oil may fall quickly; if merchant ships are attacked or US military actions escalate, oil prices, gold and military stocks may strengthen, while aviation, transportation and high energy-consuming industries will face pressure. Changes in oil prices will in turn affect U.S.  inflation and Fed interest rate expectations. The five major Wall Street banks released their rankings on Tuesday, marking the official opening of the Q2 financial reporting season. JPMorgan Chase (JPM.US), Bank of America (BAC.US), Goldman Sachs (GS.US), Wells Fargo (WFC.US), and Citigroup (C.US) will disclose their second-quarter results before the U.S.  stock market opens on Tuesday, setting the tone for this round of U.S. stock earnings season.  The market will focus on whether the investment banking business and trading income of the five major banks can benefit from active capital markets, whether net interest income continues to improve, and whether loan growth, deposit costs and credit losses signal an economic slowdown.  LSEG IBES predicts that the profit growth of S&P 500 Index (.SPX.US) constituent stocks in the second quarter may reach 23.7% year-on-year, exceeding 20% for the second consecutive quarter. If the performance and guidance of large banks remain solid, it will strengthen the judgment that the current rise in U.S.  stocks will still be supported by earnings growth; if credit quality deteriorates or cost pressures exceed expectations, it may intensify market concerns about the subsequent slowdown of the U.S. economy.  ASML and TSMC take over the rankings to test the prosperity of AI capital expenditures In addition to bank stocks, the global semiconductor industry chain will also usher in two key financial reports. ASML (ASML.US) will announce its second-quarter results before the U.S. stock market opens on Wednesday.  The market will focus on new orders, EUV equipment delivery and full-year performance guidance. As an upstream equipment supplier for global advanced process expansion, changes in ASML orders are regarded as a leading indicator of semiconductor capital expenditures. TSMC (TSM.US) will announce its complete second-quarter results before the U.S.  stock market opens on Thursday (noon Beijing time). Investors will focus on AI-related revenue, advanced process capacity utilization, advanced packaging capacity such as CoWoS, and full-year capital expenditure plans.  Whether the performance of TSMC and ASML can continue to exceed expectations will directly affect the market's judgment on the sustainability of investment in AI infrastructure. In addition, TSMC's June sales data, which was delayed due to the impact of the typhoon, will be released on Monday.  China's second quarter GDP and other data are intensively released, and Hong Kong stocks are paying attention to signs of economic recovery. China's June import and export data will be released on Tuesday.  At 10:00 on Wednesday, the State Council Information Office will hold a press conference on the performance of the national economy in the first half of the year. Data on China's second quarter GDP, total retail sales of consumer goods in June and industrial added value above designated size will be released simultaneously.  CICC predicts that economic data in June may continue to be "strong externally and weak internally", with GDP growth in the second quarter expected to be approximately 4.4% year-on-year.  For Hong Kong stocks, the degree of recovery in consumption, real estate and industrial production data will affect market expectations for subsequent stabilizing growth policies, and may lead to re-pricing of the Internet, consumption, real estate and raw material sectors.  GigaDevice welcomes tens of billions of Hong Kong dollars in lifting the ban, and the memory chip leader faces a liquidity test GigaDevice (03986.HK) will usher in the release of the first batch of cornerstone investor restricted shares after listing on July 13, involving approximately 14.39 million shares.  Based on the closing price on July 10, the market value of the unlocked shares is approximately HK$10.69 billion. Since the cumulative increase of the company's H-shares since its listing is still more than 300%, cornerstone investors hold large floating profits.  Whether there will be concentrated reductions in holdings after the lifting of the ban will become the focus of the market. As a representative company in the Hong Kong stock memory chip and domestic alternative sectors, its stock price performance may also affect the overall risk appetite of the semiconductor sector.  Technology giants have intensive activities: Huang Renxun appeared in Tokyo, Alibaba Cloud joined forces with Salesforce, Gemini 3.5 Pro is rumored to be released on Friday Next week, many star technology companies will hold product and industry events.  On July 15, NVIDIA (NVDA.US) CEO Jensen Huang will attend the 30th anniversary event of the cooperation between NVIDIA and Sega in Tokyo and demonstrate the personal AI computing platform RTX Spark to the Japanese market.  The market will pay attention to whether Huang Renxun further discloses the pace of product launch, OEM partners and personal AI computing strategy. Previously, NVIDIA had officially announced the RTX Spark product at the end of May.  On the same day, Alibaba-W (09988.HK) Alibaba Cloud and Salesforce (CRM.US) will hold an annual summit in Shanghai to discuss topics such as AI+CRM, enterprise intelligence, industry cloud and Chinese companies going global.  Some of Alibaba Cloud's DDoS protection products will also adjust their prices on the same day, and Qianwen and Doubao will simultaneously remove user-created agent functions. In addition, according to media reports, Google-C (GOOG.US) DeepMind may release Gemini 3.5 Pro on July 17.  Google has previously only confirmed that the new generation Pro model will be launched in July, but the specific date has not yet been officially confirmed.  If the new model is launched as scheduled, its code generation, agent execution capabilities and pricing will become an important basis for the market to evaluate the competitiveness of Google AI and the commercialization prospects of Google Cloud.  The World Artificial Intelligence Conference, which opens in Shanghai on July 17, will also focus on displaying Huawei's new computing products Atlas 950 SuperPoD, MINIMAX-W (00100.HK) M3 multi-modal model and a variety of AI terminals and robot products. Key performance calendar for the new week The second quarter earnings season for U.S.  stocks has officially begun. On Tuesday, the five major banks on Wall Street will focus on releasing the list. On Wednesday, attention will be paid to ASML (ASML.US), Morgan Stanley (MS.US) and BlackRock (BLK.US). On Thursday, companies such as TSMC (TSM.US), Netflix (NFLX.US), United Health (UNH.US) and Intuitive Surgical Company (ISRG.US) will take over.  As for Hong Kong stocks, there are no important corporate interim reports or specific black market trading arrangements for new shares next week. Monday (July 13) Keywords: OPEC monthly report; TSMC’s June sales data, GigaDevice lifting the ban, SK Hynix US stock leveraged ETF listing In terms of economic data, there will be no particularly heavy U.S.  economic data on Monday. In terms of financial events, OPEC will release its monthly crude oil market report. The specific time is to be determined, and it is generally released between 18:00 and 21:00 Beijing time.  In the context of the US-Iran conflict and obstruction of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz, the market will pay attention to OPEC's judgment on global crude oil supply and demand, production and price prospects.  In addition, GigaDevice (03986.HK) will usher in the release of the first batch of cornerstone investor restricted shares after listing on July 13, involving approximately 14.39 million shares. In terms of performance, TSMC (TSM.US) will release June sales data that were delayed due to the impact of the typhoon.  In terms of new stocks, SK Hynix (SKHY.US) ADR will usher in intensive listings of leveraged and inverse ETFs after its listing.  ProShares, GraniteShares, Leverage Shares and other institutions plan to launch PROSHARES ULTRA SK HYNIX (SKHU.US), 2 times long Hynix ETF-Leverage Shares (SKHX.US), 1 times short Hynix ETF-Leverage Shares (SKHZ.US), 2 times long Hynix ETF-GraniteShares (SKUU.US) and 2 times short Hynix ETF-GraniteShares (SKDD.US) and other products.  The listing of related ETFs may further amplify SK Hynix’s short-term transactions and stock price fluctuations.  Tuesday (July 14) Keywords: US CPI; Warsh’s first congressional hearing; financial reports of the five major Wall Street banks In terms of economic data, at 10:00, the State Council Information Office will hold a press conference on the import and export situation in the first half of 2026; at 18:00, the NFIB small business confidence index of the United  States in June will be released; at 20:15, the weekly changes in ADP employment in the United States for the week to June 27 will be released; at 20:30, the CPI and core CPI of the United States in June will be released.  In terms of financial events, at 00:30, Federal Reserve Board Governor Waller gave a speech; at 22:00, Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh attended the House of Representatives Financial Services Committee's "Federal Reserve Semi-Annual Monetary Policy Report" hearing.  In terms of performance, JPMorgan Chase (JPM.US), Bank of America (BAC.US), Goldman Sachs (GS.US), Wells Fargo (WFC.US), and Citigroup (C.US) will announce their results before the U.S. stock market opens.  Wednesday (July 15) Keywords: China’s second quarter GDP; US PPI; Warsh Senate hearing; ASML financial report In terms of economic data, at 09:30, the National Bureau of Statistics released a monthly report on residential sales prices in 70 large and medium-sized cities; at 10:00, China's second quarter GDP, China's total retail sales of consumer goods in  June and industrial added value above designated size were released; at 17:00, the monthly rate of industrial output in the Eurozone in May was released; at 20:30, the US PPI and core PPI in June were released.  In terms of financial events, at 01:00, Chicago Fed President Goolsby participated in a fireside chat; at 20:45, New York Fed President Williams delivered a speech; at 21:45, the Bank of Canada announced its interest rate decision and monetary policy report; at 22:00, Warsh attended the hearing of the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs; at 22:30, the Bank of Canada held a monetary policy press conference.  U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessent will also chair a meeting of the Financial Stability Oversight Committee to discuss geopolitical risks, artificial intelligence and financial stability issues. In terms of performance, ASML (ASML.US), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ.US), Morgan Stanley (MS.US), and BlackRock (BLK.US) will announce their results before the U.S.  stock market opens; United Continental Airlines (UAL.US) will release the list after the U.S. stock market opens. Thursday (July 16) Keywords: Federal Reserve Beige Book; U.S.  retail sales (USRS.EC); Bank of Korea resolution; TSMC, Netflix financial reports In terms of economic data, at 14:00, the UK's GDP, manufacturing output and industrial output data for the three months of May were released; at 20:30, the number of initial jobless claims in the United States in the week to July 11, the US June retail sales monthly rate and  the US July Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index were released; at 22:00, the US July NAHB real estate market index, May commercial inventory monthly rate and June existing home contracted sales index monthly rate were released. This Week in Foreign Interference: 94 incidents logged. The volume matters less than the pattern. The institutions meant to detect and respond to foreign interference are being hollowed out at the same moment adversaries are escalating. That combination is not a coincidence.  The highlights:  Italy, July 9. Two Russian diplomats expelled after one of them reportedly burned the identities of Italian counterintelligence officers monitoring the Russian embassy. Not a run-of-the-mill expulsion. That's a penetration.  Brazil, July 8. A Russian SVR illegal ordered deported after spending years building false identities to infiltrate organizations in the U.S. and Netherlands. Classic Directorate S methodology. The question, as always: what did he move before anyone noticed.  Taiwan, July 10. A DPP staffer named Huang sentenced to ten years for espionage. He was running CCP agents inside the ruling party and successfully turned one of two targets. The court treated it seriously. The LDP is advancing a foreign interference law that would allow warrantless communications interception. Japan has historically lacked this authority entirely, which Chinese and Russian services have exploited for years. Civil liberties opposition is real. So is the gap.  Iceland's EU referendum warning came during the NATO Summit. The Foreign Minister flagged Russian interference risk explicitly. Binary referenda on high-stakes geopolitical questions are among the most vulnerable formats. Watch that one develop.  Beyond the highlights: a dozen more China-linked espionage and influence items this week, including Beijing's glorification of a physical attack on pro-Taiwan Japanese commentator Akio Yaita and the EU Commission's draft procurement exclusion framework targeting Chinese and Russian state-linked firms. North Korea's Lazarus Group added botnet and DLL injection capabilities in the latest Famous Chollima wave. Russia's Wagner-linked disinformation machine completed France's expulsion from Burkina Faso. The historical items in this week's log, Glenn Souther's 1986 defection, the 1996 FBI spy arrest, the 1983 Soviet West German election operation, are worth reading as baseline: the methods are older than the internet. In February 2025, Bybit lost $1.5 billion in Ethereum the largest crypto heist ever recorded.   North Korea's Lazarus Group didn't crack any cryptography.   They compromised a developer's machine at Safe, Bybit's multisig platform, and altered the interface so a routine transfer looked normal.   Bybit's team signed what they saw on screen.   What they actually approved rerouted 401,000 ETH straight into the attackers' wallets. $160 million was laundered within 48 hours.  Bybit wasn't careless.   The hack exposed a deeper hole: sign and trust what you see can be faked, and once funds move on a public ledger, everyone including the thief's own tracking tools can watch exactly where they go, forever.  That's the problem Umbra was built to solve, and it's why I've spent the past week actually using it instead of just reading the deck.  ✓ What @UmbraPrivacy actually does  Umbra is a self-custodial, Solana native wallet with privacy built into the foundation, not bolted on as an afterthought.   You keep your own keys, Umbra never touches your seed phrase but the way your money moves changes completely.  There's a Shield feature that takes your visible, on-chain balance and routes it into a private state.   Once shielded, your holdings stop showing up as a neat public ledger entry that anyone can total up.  From there, shielded transfers use zero-knowledge proofs to hide the three things that usually give you away:   who sent it,   who received it,   and how much moved.   Umbra runs on Arcium's encrypted execution network, so computation happens on encrypted data directly.   Private swaps work the same way. You can trade without broadcasting your position size or your entry to every MEV bot and copy-trader watching the mempool.  ✓ The feature that actually got me  Here's the part I didn't expect to care about.   I've had the "what if someone forces me to unlock my wallet" thought before, sitting in traffic with my phone out, doing a transfer at an agent location.  Every crypto user I know has run that scenario in their head at least once and had no real answer for it.  Umbra has Distress Mode.  You can set up a secondary wallet view that shows a limited, decoy balance.   If you're ever pressured, a physical threat, someone leaning on you at an ATM, you unlock into the decoy, not your real funds.  That's the first time I've seen a wallet design for the moment things go wrong in the real world, not just the moment a hacker tries something on-chain.   Most security features in this space are audits and multisigs important, but abstract.  Distress Mode is the first one that felt like it was built for someone who actually has to walk home with their phone in their pocket.  ✓ Why this matters for people like us, specifically  In most of the markets I write for, crypto isn't a trading hobby. It's rent, it's stock for a small business, it's a way to get paid by a client abroad.   A fully public ledger means a landlord, a business rival, or a stranger at a bus stop can look up what you're worth.  That's a physical safety and negotiating-leverage problem, and it's exactly what Distress Mode is answering.  Umbra doesn't ask you to trade away speed or usability to get privacy, it still runs on Solana, so you keep sub-second finality and near-zero fees.  It also keeps a compliance layer (viewing keys, risk screening) so privacy doesn't mean opting out of every legitimate rail.  It's live now on web, iOS, Android, and as a browser extension, no waitlist, no phased rollout excuse.  Questions this piece answers  1. What happened in the Bybit hack, and what does it reveal about crypto "security"?  2. What is Umbra and how does shielding a balance work?  3. How do private transfers and swaps hide sender, recipient, and amount?  4. What is Distress Mode, and why did it become the standout feature?  5. Does using Umbra mean giving up Solana's speed or low fees?  @ownershipfm

The man in front of me at the gate had been limping so hard for twenty minutes that I started feeling bad for being annoyed.  Then they announced preboarding.  He straightened up like Lazarus.  Not fully. Just enough to move fast.  Gate Agent: Anyone needing extra time or assistance may board now.  Him: That's me.  He was suddenly first in line.  The woman behind me leaned over.  Woman: Funny how healing works.  I tried not to laugh.  Then the gate agent looked at him, looked at his dramatic limp, and said the most devastating thing possible.  Gate Agent: Of course, sir. We can get you a wheelchair for your connection too.  He froze.  Him: I don't need all that.  Gate Agent: It's no trouble.  Him: It's a short limp.  Gate Agent: Better safe than sorry.  His wife, who had been pretending not to know him morally, finally stepped in.  Wife: Gary.  Him: What?  Wife: Take the wheelchair.  Him: I don't need a wheelchair.  Wife: Then maybe you didn't need to be first in line either.  The gate agent kept smiling that customer-service smile people use when they know they're winning.  Gate Agent: Wheelchair assistance can meet you plane-side in Dallas. What gate are you connecting to?  Gary: I can walk in Dallas.  Wife: Interesting.  Gary: Linda.  Wife: No, say it louder. Maybe your leg will hear you.  Now everyone in our section was listening in the open. Nobody even pretended otherwise.  Me: (to the woman behind me) This is better than boarding.  Woman: Best thing that's happened in Terminal C all week.  Gary tried one last time.  Gary: I just didn't want to wait.  Gate Agent: And now you won't have to.  She printed something. Attached it to his boarding pass.  Gate Agent: There we go. Full assistance noted.  Wife: Perfect.  Gary: Linda, cancel it.  Wife: I don't work here, Gary.  He boarded with the preboard group, no wheelchair yet, but the damage was done.  When we landed in Dallas, there was a guy waiting at the jet bridge with a chair.  Airport Attendant: Gary?  Whole front half of the plane turned.  Gary looked like he wanted the earth to absorb him.  Wife: That's you, sweetheart.  He sat in the chair.  He had to.  As they rolled him away, the woman behind me whispered:  Woman: Miracles do happen.  I hope Gary made his connection.  I know his pride didn't.

Russia's FSB targeted a NATO member's civilian power grid in the middle of winter, with roughly 500,000 people in the potential blast radius. The attack failed. Today — seven months later — the EU and UK stopped hedging and said so on the record.  The formal attribution, published July 13, lands on FSB Centre 16: Russia's elite signals intelligence and cyber operations division, distinct from APT28 and APT29, and increasingly operational in a way that goes well beyond intelligence collection. The weapon was DynoWiper, a new destructive wiper designed specifically to sever communications between renewable energy hardware and power distribution operators. Poland's grid stayed up. The intent is the story.  DynoWiper isn't a departure — it's a refinement. The wiper lineage runs directly through Sandworm's CaddyWiper campaign against Ukrainian utilities in 2023 and back to WhisperGate at the start of Russia's 2022 invasion: disrupt ICS/SCADA communication layers, create cascading operational failures, erase your forensic trail on the way out. What's new here is the target selection. Renewable integration infrastructure — smart inverters, DERMS, SCADA-to-renewable interfaces — is newer stack, less hardened, and less monitored than legacy grid SCADA. FSB Centre 16 noticed. That's not a coincidence; it's a documented evolution in ICS targeting doctrine.  The six-month gap between Poland's January confirmation and today's formal attribution is not bureaucratic drift. It's the operational tempo of Five Eyes-adjacent attribution work: you don't publish until you've exhausted SIGINT collection value, confirmed the forensic chain, and pre-staged the diplomatic response package. Today's announcement came with sweeping EU and UK sanctions and a co-authored NCSC technical advisory published simultaneously. That's a coordinated release, not a press conference.  The NCSC advisory is the part that actually matters operationally. The diplomatic attribution is historically significant — the West publicly naming FSB Centre 16 as a destructive threat actor against NATO civilian infrastructure changes escalation calculus on both sides of that line. But the advisory is the directive. It updates Russia's documented tradecraft against energy infrastructure and carries urgent guidance for critical infrastructure operators across Europe. Any energy sector security team reading the wire today should be treating it as an action item, not background reading.  The MITRE mapping tells its own story: T0831 (Manipulation of Control — disrupting renewable-to-distribution communications), T0829 (Loss of View — DynoWiper consistent with destructive ICS payload class), T0838 (Inhibit Response Function — targeting the communication layer specifically to prevent operator intervention). The attack was designed to blind operators and hold them there. The grid held anyway, but the architecture of the attack shows exactly where Centre 16 is probing next.  For European critical infrastructure operators, the NCSC advisory is not optional reading. Review network segmentation between IT and OT environments now, specifically at renewable integration points. Deferred patch windows need to close. This is an active targeting environment. The formal attribution changes the political record; the advisory changes what you do Monday morning.  Seven months to say it out loud. The FSB noticed Tuesday. We are nothing if not consistent.  Source: The Register — Connor Jones, 15:39 UTC, July 13, 2026. FCDO attribution statement and NCSC Joint Technical Advisory both published today.

불개미라는 KOL은 험한 한국 가상자산 시장에서 등불같은 KOL이라는 생각이 듭니다. 러그가 판치는 세상에서 그래도 자신 만의 기준을 가지고 투자자들에게 좋은 투자 정보와 기회를 제공하기 때문이죠.   DSCVR 프로젝트에 대해서 2주 전에 안보이는 것이 보이기 시작한다는 주제로 쇼츠가 올라왔었고 그 정보를 바탕으로 글을 작성했던 기억이 있습니다.   주요한 내용은  1. 단순한 소셜을 넘어선 '앱 배포 플랫폼' 소셜 기반 배포 레이어: 피드 내에서 AI 에이전트, Web3 앱, 인터랙티브 콘텐츠가 실시간으로 작동  강력한 개발자 생태계: Canvas 프레임워크, API, SDK를 지원하여 외부 앱을 피드 안에 손쉽게 통합  새로운 소셜 패러다임: 크리에이터와 팬이 플랫폼을 '공동 소유'하는 Web3 가치를 지향  2. 탄탄한 투자 배경 (Backers) 총 조달 금액: $9M (약 120억 원 규모, Seed 라운드 / 2022-07-25)  리드 투자자: 크립토 업계의 거물 Polychain Capital  참여 VC: Shima Capital, Upfront Ventures, Tomahawk VC, Fyrfly Venture Partners, BDMI 등 탑티어 투자사 참여  한다는 내용이었습니다. 이번에는 우리가 많이 알고 있는 명탐정 코난을 모티브로 한 1분 내외의 쇼츠 영상이 올라왔는데   먼저, 동영상을 전체적으로 살펴봤을 때의 느낀 점과 주요한 내용 그리고 이것을 통해 어떠한 투자를 할 수 있을지에 대한 아이디어에 대해서 기술해보고자 합니다.   -줄거리- 잘 나가던 크립토 트레이더였떤 불개미는 러그풀, 해킹 등으로  자산을 잃고 진정한 알파를 찾기 위한 여정에 나선다. 그 때 DSCVR 박사님에 의해 DSCVR에 대해서 알게 되고 여러 온체인 커뮤니티의 실시간 모니터링이 가능한 AI tracker 기능, 관심있는 프로젝트나 주제를 입력하면 리포트를 제공하는 AI Discovery,  $DS 토큰을 스테이킹하면 사용할 수 있는 프리미엄 기능(더 깊이 있는 AI 분석, 더 빠른 알림이 가능)을 통해 알파를 찾게 되었다.   -느낀점- 먼저 불개미 커뮤니티에서 쇼츠가 계속 나오고 있는데 퀄리티가 점점 더 좋아지는 느낌입니다. AI 동영상 플랫폼의 퀄리티가 좋아져서 그럴 수도 있지만 시나리오를 보고 고민한 흔적이 느껴졌고 그래서 프로젝트 팀에서 굉장히 좋아하지 않을까 하는 생각이 들었습니다.   그리고 여러 장면 중에서 인상깊었던 건 첨부한 사진처럼 'Web3의 불룸버그 터미널' 이라는 것이었습니다. 불룸버그는 Web2에서 팩트체크 분야로 나름 알려진 언론인데 DSCVR이 불룸버그의 역할을 한다는 문구가 이 프로젝트의 성격을 규정짓는 좋은 문구가 아닐까 하는 생각이 들었습니다.   더불어서 DSCVR이 프로젝트나 주제를 입력하면 제공하는 리포트가 있다는 내용에서 Surf가 생각이 났습니다. 이건 사용할 때  어떤 식으로 비용을 제공하게 될지 그런 생각이 들었습니다.  -투자 계획-  먼저 AI Tracker와 AI Discovery의 기능을 통해 커뮤니티의 의견이 무엇보다도 중요하고 빠른 의사 결정이 필요한 밈코인 영역에서 적극적으로 사용하게 될 것 같습니다.   또한, 더 정확한 의견을 받기 위해서 DS 토큰을 스테이킹해서 사용할 거 같기는 합니다. 개인적으로는 어떤 양으로 스테이킹했을 때 정확도가 향상되는지 특정 갯수의 캡이 있는지 이런 궁금증이 들긴 합니다.   또한 사용할 때의 비용이 어느 정도 드는지 그런 궁금증도 있습니다.

⚠️ 국제 보안업계와 한미 정부가 북한 정찰총국 연계 산하기관의 해킹 조직으로 알려진 '김수키(Kimsuky)'의 활동을 지속적으로 분석하고 경고해 왔습니다.  북한 "김수키" 글로벌 해킹조직,  한국 정부·기업을 지속적으로 표적으로 삼아 국방부·통신사 등 핵심 기관을 공격하고, 중국 해커들과 기술을 공유한 정황 포착.  국가 기밀급 자료가 유출됐을 가능성도 제기. 싱크탱크·연구자·정부 관계자 집중 공략. 스피어 피싱 + 악성코드로 정보 탈취. 최근 글로벌(미국·유럽)로 확대.  ➡️2013. 카스퍼스키 보고서(러시아 사이버 보안업체)  -'kimsuky'최초 명명-악성코드, C2, 이메일 "The 'kimsuky' Operation: A North Korean APT?" securelist.com/the-kimsuky-o…  ➡️2019. 이스트시큐리티 보고서(한국 보안업체) -"'Kimsuky & Kommi' 조직의 공통점 발견" estsecurity.com/enterprise/s…  ➡️2023. 한국 미국 정부 합동 보안 권고문 "북한 해킹 조직, 군사 외교 정보 수집과 정권의 군사, 핵무기 개발을 지원하기 위한 글로벌 해킹 활동"경고 공동 발표. nsa.gov/Press-Room/Press-Rel…  ➡️2025. 프랙에 실린 보고서, "APT Down: The North Korea Files"(북한의 지능형 지속 공격) -한국 국방·외교·원자력·통신 분야에 대한 지속적 침투 정황. 중국 해커와의 도구 공유 의혹까지 제기되며 글로벌 위협으로 확대된다고 분석. biz.chosun.com/it-science/ic…   ⚠️지금 당장 주의하세요 ❗️정부기관과 외교·안보 분야를 주요 표적으로 삼음 ❗️스피어피싱과 사회공학 기법을 적극 활용 ->출처 불명의 이메일·첨부파일 절대 열지 마세요 ❗️장기간 잠복하며 정보 수집을 시도하는 APT 방식 ❗️공격 기법이 지속적으로 고도화되는 추세  국가 안보를 위협하는 사이버 공격, 우리 모두의 경각심이 필요합니다. #김수키 #Kimsuky #북한해킹 #사이버안보 #국가안보

Grok Build is straight up malware behavior with an Apple notarized signature.  You could explicitly deny the agent permission to read a file and it would upload that exact file anyway, because a separate code path silently packed your entire repo, full Git history included, and shipped it to an xAI controlled Google Cloud Storage bucket. The captured bundle was valid, cloneable, and contained the fake API credential the agent was forbidden from touching.  Not a sketchy community fork either. The binary is Apple signed by X.AI Corporation and literally contains strings for grok code session traces, after_codebase.tar.gz, and disable_codebase_upload.  And it's not fully quiet even now. With /privacy active and trace_upload_enabled=false, the client still fired a 7,407 byte request to /v1/traces.  Recap: a binary ignored your file access policy, serialized your whole repo including deleted history and credentials, exfiltrated it through traffic documented as ordinary updates, and only stopped when a remote flag got flipped. If any random executable did this you would call it malware. No exceptions for a signature.  The historical upload is independently verified from preserved public evidence. Live retest confirms repo upload is currently server disabled for the tested account while trace egress continues. All credentials shown are fake canaries.  Receipts: github.com/arafatkatze/grok-…

#threatreport #LowCompleteness Compromised npm Packages in the AsyncAPI Namespace Deliver Miasma Botnet Loader | 14-07-2026 Source: socket.dev/blog/asyncapi-sup… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Miasma, Credential_harvesting_technique,  🎯Victims: Developers, Continuous integration environments  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1027, T1036, T1059, T1059.007, T1105, T1195.001  🧨IOCs:  - File: 1  - Url: 1  - IP: 1  💽Software: Node.js, macOS, Linux, claude, vscode  🔠Functions: require  #threatreport: The Threat Research Team at Socket has uncovered three compromised npm packages within the @asyncapi namespace: @asyncapi/generator-helpers (version 1.1.1), @asyncapi/generator-components (version 0.7.1), and @asyncapi/generator (version 3.3.1). These packages have been exploited to distribute a multi-stage botnet loader. The first stage of this malicious activity involves an obfuscated payload that, upon execution, downloads an encrypted second-stage payload known as Miasma from the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS).  The compromised AsyncAPI packages are commonly used in development workflows for generating API documentation and client code based on AsyncAPI definitions. As these packages can be invoked during regular development or continuous integration (CI) processes, the malicious payload operates without needing a specific install hook to execute. The threat is encapsulated in the file `src/utils.js`, where the malicious code is cleverly concealed behind excessive whitespace. This code executes at the point of `require()`, which initiates a detached Node.js child process that is responsible for downloading the second-stage payload.  The second stage of the malware is saved as `sync.js` within platform-specific directories and is disguised as a legitimate Node.js file. The initial obfuscation technique utilizes a spawn command to download the second stage, exemplified by the following code snippet: `const { spawn } = require('child_process'); spawn ('node' ,  '-e' , downloadStage2Code , { detached : true , stdio : 'ignore' , windowsHide : true }).unref ();`.  Current analysis indicates that this decrypted second-stage payload constitutes a botnet framework equipped with various capabilities, including the execution of shell commands, file operations, credential harvesting, evasion techniques, persistence across reboots, and multi-protocol command-and-control (C2) functionalities. Users of the affected packages are advised to avoid these versions, ensure they upgrade to patched releases when they become available, and review both their development and CI environments for any signs of compromise.

Fake Windows Update page delivering a multi-stage malware loader.  The site microsoft[.]updata[.]net[.]cn instructs users to run a PowerShell command that downloads kB2025072926.exe from a Cloudflare R2 bucket.  Dynamic analysis shows kB2025072926.exe first deploys a copy of the legitimate open-source TrafficMonitor application under %AppData%\Roaming\TrafficMonitor\ (including its FAQ shortcut pointing to the official GitHub project).   It then drops a second stage under C:\ProgramData\SystemTools\,  including:  - xaweg.exe - pnMpWI32.dll - comsdk.dll - Cache.pmt  xaweg.exe loads pnMpWI32.dll, which reads Cache.pmt and appears to decrypt and/or decompress it before executing shellcode in memory.  Interestingly, a @urlscanio scan from 2025 on the same fake update site shows it previously delivered WindowsUpdata.exe (SHA256: a71455506d7048f1dbbcdd48100860a8cd2088cbe4a0b3881cc8a179ce494e99), which the community associated with Amadey and Black Basta. The current sample follows a different infection chain, so further analysis is required before attributing it to the same malware family.  IOCs:  microsoft[.]updata[.]net[.]cn pub-620def1d93ef431dbdf24616a9b11c77[.]r2[.]dev/kB2025072926.exe %AppData%\Roaming\TrafficMonitor\ C:\ProgramData\SystemTools\xaweg.exe C:\ProgramData\SystemTools\pnMpWI32.dll C:\ProgramData\SystemTools\Cache.pmt  Hash:   Filename: xaweg.exe 35ae900295477f1c47edc229cd08c049fa50c1c14fab9f308868027134db778d  Filename: pnMpWI32.dll 7bb5f352f582407330bba12d2ddd15e70a3c5002fe96b7366980e644bec8a565  Filename: Cache.pmt 5a514fb0caceb37000a716df626163f5f8856bc7703adfa2bc3c1b59103c4ff4  Filename: kB2025072926.exe b127701d2be865e3dc9417f6e0975e73b55b44906f166dc7147c9b392d008a00  #Malware #Fakeupdate #Clickfix #Threathunting @malwrhunterteam @500mk500 @skocherhan @JAMESWT_WT @James_inthe_box

A hacker broke into Injective Labs' official GitHub last week and slipped malware into a routine code update. It quietly copied wallet seed phrases the second a wallet loaded — disguised as normal traffic so it wouldn't get flagged.  Team caught it and shipped a fix in 49 minutes. No funds lost. The real lesson: even a legit, well-known project can get compromised one level up, in code you never see or approve.  Your seed phrase shouldn't live anywhere that auto-updates without you knowing. A hardware wallet keeps your keys offline — this exact attack can't reach them there.  Want the deeper breakdown? TOPIC 1 OF 4: Directed Energy vs. the Drone Swarm — Post 1 of 3 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗔𝗥𝗜𝗧𝗛𝗠𝗘𝗧𝗜𝗖 𝗢𝗙 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗗𝗥𝗢𝗡𝗘 𝗪𝗔𝗥: WHY THE PENTAGON IS BUILDING RAY GUNS 𝘛𝘰𝘱𝘪𝘤 1 𝘰𝘧 4 — 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘓𝘰𝘺𝘢𝘭 𝘞𝘪𝘯𝘨𝘮𝘢𝘯 𝘚𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘦𝘴: Topic 1: Directed Energy vs. the Drone Swarm · Topic 2: The Supersonic Wingman Economics · Topic 3: Printing the Wingman: A Reality Check · Topic 4: Who Actually Has the Exotic-Materials Printing Edge For three years the cheap drone has been winning. A $20,000 to $50,000 Shahed-type loitering munition, or a sub-$500 FPV quadcopter rigged with a warhead, has been trading favorably against tanks, radars, and even warships. [FACT] Iranian-made Shahed-136 drones, and the domestically produced Russian variant known as Geran-2, cost in the range of $20,000 to $50,000 per unit, while the US has been intercepting them with Patriot missiles costing $3 million to $4 million and THAAD interceptors costing roughly $10 million apiece. That is not a defense posture. That is a subsidy program running in the wrong direction, and every officer who has had to sign off on a fire-control order knows it. I have spent a career living by the discipline of P&L accountability — if the unit economics don’t work, the program doesn’t survive contact with a budget committee, let alone with an enemy who has figured out your cost structure and is exploiting it on purpose. That is exactly what is happening in the skies over Ukraine, the Red Sea, and now the Gulf. The question this piece answers is simple: is the United States, and the West more broadly, actually closing that cost gap, or are we still writing million-dollar checks to kill twenty-thousand-dollar problems? 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗧𝗛𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗧 𝗣𝗜𝗖𝗧𝗨𝗥𝗘: WHAT’S ACTUALLY FLYING Start with what has proven itself in combat, because a lot of drone journalism confuses “unveiled” with “fielded.” [JUDGMENT] I am drawing a hard line in this piece between systems that have been combat-tested and systems that remain demonstrators, because conflating the two is exactly the kind of error that gets people killed and budgets wasted. The General Atomics MQ-9 Reaper and its maritime-oriented MQ-9B Sky Guardian variant remain the gold standard for persistent, high-endurance strike and ISR — endurance north of 24 hours, payloads in excess of a ton and a half, and a combat record stretching from Afghanistan through Iraq, Syria, and Yemen. [FACT] Turkey’s Bayraktar TB2, and its newer jet-powered stablemate the Bayraktar Kızılelma, represent the opposite end of the spectrum: cheap, rugged, operable from unimproved strips, and combat-proven in Nagorno-Karabakh in 2020, in Libya, and in the early phase of the Russia-Ukraine war when Russian air defense and electronic warfare coverage was thin. Roughly 600 TB2s have been produced and the type has been widely exported. [FACT] Below those two tiers sits the mass — the loitering munitions and FPV drones that are doing the actual killing at scale in Ukraine. The Russian Lancet is a precision, top-attack loitering munition that has proven highly effective against armor and artillery. Iranian-designed Shahed-136 drones, built domestically in Russia as the Geran-2, are cheap, one-way saturation weapons. On the Ukrainian side, mass-produced FPV drones — both radio-controlled and, increasingly, fiber-optic guided — have become the single largest killer on the battlefield, with various open-source estimates putting drone-caused casualties at a majority share of total casualties in the war. [INFERENCE — the exact casualty percentage varies by source and methodology; treat any single figure, including the commonly cited “70-plus percent,” as an estimate, not a hard count] Both sides in Ukraine are now producing drones in numbers that dwarf the production runs of virtually any other nation’s arsenal. [FACT] That is the mass side of the equation. The quality side is a separate and newer development: fiber-optic guided drones, which unspool a literal thread of glass fiber behind them as they fly, giving the operator a physical, un-jammable link to the aircraft. Because there is no radio signal between pilot and drone, conventional electronic warfare — which works by jamming or spoofing that radio link — is completely blind to them. Ukraine’s Defense Minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, has stated publicly that Russian forces are now fielding fiber-optic FPV drones with roughly 31 miles of range, which he has called a serious and growing threat to logistics far behind the front line. [FACT] That is the crux of the problem this piece is about. When you can’t jam the link, you have exactly two options left: destroy the airframe kinetically, or fry the electronics onboard the drone directly, regardless of what link is or isn’t connected to them. The second option is directed energy, and it has moved from laboratory demonstrator to fielded weapon system faster than almost any other category of military technology in the last five years. 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗥𝗘𝗗 𝗦𝗘𝗔 𝗔𝗡𝗗 𝗜𝗥𝗔𝗡 𝗖𝗔𝗦𝗘 𝗦𝗧𝗨𝗗𝗬: WHERE THE COST PROBLEM GOT UNDENIABLE The theoretical cost-asymmetry argument became an operational crisis in two theaters almost simultaneously. In the Red Sea, Houthi forces used cheap Iranian-supplied drones and anti-ship missiles against commercial shipping and US Navy escorts for an extended period, forcing American destroyers to expend interceptor missiles that cost, in some cases, many multiples of the value of the target they were shooting down. Then, in the 2026 conflict involving Iran, the same math repeated itself at a larger scale: US forces were shooting down Iranian Shahed-136 drones — costing an estimated $20,000 to $50,000 each — using Patriot interceptors priced at $3 million to $4 million and THAAD interceptors priced at roughly $10 million. [FACT] A Trump administration official put the total cost of that conflict at roughly $12 billion over its first weeks, or nearly $1 billion a day — a figure driven in no small part by exactly this kind of unfavorable exchange ratio. [FACT] It was in the immediate aftermath of that deployment that HELIOS aboard USS Preble, operating off the coast of Iran, logged confirmed successful engagements against incoming drone threats, per Lockheed Martin’s own public statements and February 2026 testing disclosures. [FACT] This is not an abstract acquisition-strategy argument anymore. It is a live-fire lesson that the Navy, the Army, and Congress have all absorbed in real time, and it is the direct cause of the funding acceleration behind Leonidas, JLWS, and the containerized HELIOS push discussed below. [JUDGMENT] I would go further: this is the clearest demonstration in a generation of why “we can always outspend the enemy” is not a strategy, it is a hope, and hope is not a method — a phrase I learned the hard way in aerospace program management, where an unfavorable unit-cost trendline does not fix itself. It gets fixed by the people willing to change the engineering, not the people willing to keep signing the same purchase order. [OPINION] 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗚𝗢𝗟𝗗𝗘𝗡 𝗗𝗢𝗠𝗘 𝗖𝗢𝗡𝗡𝗘𝗖𝗧𝗜𝗢𝗡 Lockheed Martin has explicitly positioned its HELIOS demonstrations as supporting evidence for the broader Golden Dome homeland missile and drone defense initiative — the layered, continental-scale defense architecture the administration has been pushing to protect the homeland against drone incursions and cruise missiles, with an eye eventually toward ballistic and hypersonic threats. [FACT] Directed energy is one layer of that architecture, specifically the layer meant to handle the high-volume, low-cost threat category — exactly the drones this piece has been discussing — so that the exquisite, expensive interceptor layers are preserved for genuinely high-end threats. [INFERENCE] Whether Golden Dome ultimately fields containerized HELIOS or JLWS derivatives at meaningful scale, and on what timeline, remains an open program-execution question rather than a settled fact, and I will not pretend otherwise. Defense programs of this scope have a well-documented history of schedule slip, and nothing about directed energy’s underlying physics exempts it from that history. [JUDGMENT] 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗘𝗪 𝗦𝗛𝗘𝗟𝗙 𝗟𝗜𝗙𝗘 𝗣𝗥𝗢𝗕𝗟𝗘𝗠 Before getting to directed energy, it’s worth being honest about why traditional electronic warfare — jamming and spoofing — has a shrinking half-life. A jamming frequency deployed today against Russian or Ukrainian drone control links is typically defeated within roughly three weeks by a frequency-hop or software update on the adversary’s side. [INFERENCE — this “three week” figure circulates widely in open-source battlefield reporting from Ukraine but should be read as an approximate, theater-specific observation rather than a universal law of EW]. That arms race between jammer and drone software has been running continuously since 2022, and it is precisely what pushed both sides toward fiber-optic guidance in the first place — an engineering answer to an EW problem that simply removes the RF link the jammer depends on. This matters for anyone trying to build a defense architecture, because it means EW alone is not a durable answer. It buys time. It does not win the arms race by itself. That is the gap directed energy is being built to fill. 𝗗𝗜𝗥𝗘𝗖𝗧𝗘𝗗 𝗘𝗡𝗘𝗥𝗚𝗬, 𝗣𝗔𝗥𝗧 𝗢𝗡𝗘: 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗟𝗔𝗦𝗘𝗥 — HELIOS AND THE SPEED-OF-LIGHT INTERCEPT The most mature shipboard laser in the US arsenal is Lockheed Martin’s HELIOS — High Energy Laser with Integrated Optical-dazzler and Surveillance, officially designated Mk 5 Mod 0. [FACT] It is a 60-kilowatt-class fiber laser, built with a modular architecture the Navy says can be expanded toward the 120 to 150-kilowatt range, and it is fully integrated into the Aegis Combat System aboard the guided-missile destroyer USS Preble (DDG-88), the only ship in the fleet currently equipped with it. HELIOS has been installed on Preble since 2022. Seven other Arleigh Burke-class destroyers carry the lower-powered ODIN system, which can dazzle and blind an incoming threat’s optical sensors but cannot physically destroy it — HELIOS can do both. The most important operational data point, and one worth stating precisely because so much drone-war reporting rounds it up or down for effect: in a counter-UAS demonstration at sea, HELIOS successfully engaged and neutralized four separate drone threats in a single exercise. [FACT] Lockheed Martin CEO Jim Taiclet confirmed the four-drone engagement publicly, describing it as proof that the system could conserve the ship’s finite, expensive interceptor missiles — a single RIM-116 Rolling Airframe Missile costs roughly $1 million — for higher-tier threats, while HELIOS handles the cheap stuff at a marginal cost the Navy and outside analysts have pegged at roughly $1 to $10 per shot in electricity. [FACT] That is the correct way to understand what “four drones” means. It is not a swarm-defeat number — a laser is a single-target-at-a-time weapon, engaging targets sequentially, not simultaneously — but it is real, disclosed, operational data from an active-duty warship, not a lab demonstration, and that distinguishes it from most of what gets called a “directed energy breakthrough” in the trade press. [JUDGMENT] I want to be direct about a limitation the enthusiast coverage tends to bury: the Navy has not publicly disclosed HELIOS’s engagement range or the time-to-kill on those four engagements, and outside observers, including The War Zone’s defense desk, have noted that a genuinely complete picture of the system’s operational tempo is still not public. Treat range and speed claims for HELIOS beyond “operationally demonstrated, multi-target capable” as classified or unknown, not as zero. The physical limitations are real and worth stating plainly, because they are the reason directed energy is a layer, not a replacement for the missile inventory: salt spray, fog, smoke, and dust scatter and attenuate the beam, shrinking effective range in bad weather; a 60-kilowatt beam needs several seconds of dwell time to defeat a target, which is fine against a slow drone and useless against a supersonic anti-ship cruise missile; and running a 60-kilowatt laser alongside a power-hungry AN/SPY-6 air defense radar on a Flight III Arleigh Burke destroyer requires real power-management engineering, not just plugging in a bigger battery. [FACT/INFERENCE — the specific “several seconds” dwell-time figure is a widely cited industry approximation for 50-to-100kW-class systems against small UAS, not a disclosed HELIOS specification] Where this is headed: Congress is pushing the Navy toward a containerized “laser-in-a-box” variant of the HELIOS technology stack, which could be dropped onto an amphibious ship, a carrier flight deck, or a shore site without the multi-year integration effort that putting a laser into a destroyer’s hull required. [FACT] Lessons from HELIOS are also feeding a joint Army-Navy program called the Joint Laser Weapon System (JLWS), aimed at scaling from the 60-kilowatt tier toward 150-kilowatt and eventually 300-kilowatt-class containerized systems, which is roughly the power tier at which dwell time drops low enough to threaten cruise missiles, not just drones. The Navy’s proposed budget has already included a $75.6 million line item for JLWS development. [FACT] Outside the US Navy, Israel’s Iron Beam ground-based laser has moved from demonstrator into active service as a complement to the kinetic Iron Dome system, intercepting rockets, mortars, and drones over fixed sites and borders. The UK’s DragonFire, a 50-kilowatt-class laser built by MBDA, has advanced through night-fire testing toward operational deployment, and the Royal Navy has committed a $430 million contract to MBDA to equip Type 45 destroyers with the system. [FACT] China and Russia both maintain active laser weapons development and testing programs; public detail on Russian operational status in particular remains limited and should be treated with appropriate skepticism rather than taken at face value from state media claims. [JUDGMENT] 𝗗𝗜𝗥𝗘𝗖𝗧𝗘𝗗 𝗘𝗡𝗘𝗥𝗚𝗬, 𝗣𝗔𝗥𝗧 𝗧𝗪𝗢: 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗠𝗜𝗖𝗥𝗢𝗪𝗔𝗩𝗘 — LEONIDAS AND THE SWARM PROBLEM A laser is a sniper rifle. It kills one target at a time, precisely, but sequentially. If the actual threat is a swarm of forty or fifty cheap drones arriving in the same sixty seconds, sequential engagement is the wrong tool, no matter how fast the laser can retarget. That is the gap high-power microwave (HPM) weapons are built to close, and it is the technology that most directly answers the question this piece opened with: can you build a weapon that kills multiple drones at once and genuinely breaks the cost-per-kill math? The answer, based on disclosed testing, is yes, with real caveats. Epirus’s Leonidas platform is the most mature HPM system in US service, built on software-defined, solid-state gallium-nitride (GaN) amplifiers that let an operator either focus a tight, precise beam on a single target or open up a wide-area cone to blanket an entire sector. [FACT] Unlike a laser, which burns through material, Leonidas works by flooding a drone’s onboard wiring with a high-voltage electromagnetic spike, overloading and frying the flight controller, GPS receiver, and other sensitive electronics almost instantly, regardless of the weather. Two disclosed test results define what Leonidas can currently do. First, in a swarm-defeat demonstration, the system brought down 49 drones simultaneously in a single engagement. [FACT] Second, and more significant for the fiber-optic threat discussed above: in December 2025, at a US government test site, the smaller Leonidas Vehicle Kit (VK) variant — mounted on a pickup truck and roughly a third the size of the full system — successfully disabled a fiber-optic guided FPV drone, marking, according to Epirus, the first known instance of a directed-energy weapon defeating a drone that had no radio link to jam in the first place. [FACT] That is the technical breakthrough that matters most in this piece: it demonstrates that HPM does not care whether the drone is radio-controlled, fiber-optic guided, or running an autonomous AI targeting routine, because it attacks the silicon, not the signal. Leonidas is already fielded, not just tested. The US Army has procured it as the Indirect Fire Protection Capability-High-Power Microwave (IFPC-HPM) system since 2023, with more than $120 million in contracts awarded to date, including a further $43.5 million contract in mid-2025, and Epirus has partnered with General Dynamics Land Systems to integrate the array onto a driverless Autonomous Ground Vehicle (AGV) chassis and onto the Stryker platform for mobile, forward-deployed protection. [FACT] The system is rated against small UAS in the 27-kilogram class and below, operating in swarm scenarios. Other HPM programs worth tracking: the Air Force Research Laboratory’s THOR (Tactical High-power Operational Responder), a containerized system sized for C-130 transport, and an Army prototype code-named Honey Badger, both aimed at establishing a standardized HPM procurement pipeline alongside commercial options like Leonidas. [FACT] China has publicly unveiled its own mobile HPM systems, the NORINCO Hurricane 2000 (mounted on an 8x8 armored vehicle for mobile field air defense) and the larger Hurricane 3000 (built on heavy trucks for fixed-site and infrastructure defense). India’s Defence Research and Development organisation has validated a prototype generating 450 megawatts of peak power in 20-nanosecond pulses, with a stated drone-kill range out to roughly 5 kilometers. [FACT — as disclosed by DRDO; independent verification of Indian government defense claims should be treated with the same caution applied to any single-source national claim] The honest limitations: HPM disperses in a wide cone and does not discriminate between friend and foe, so a friendly drone caught in the beam gets fried along with the enemy’s — Leonidas addresses this with software-defined beam-steering to carve out safe corridors, but the fratricide risk does not disappear, it is managed. Shielding is also a real counter-measure on the horizon: hardening a drone’s electronics inside even a rudimentary Faraday cage using conductive shielding can blunt or absorb an HPM pulse, forcing the weapon to run at higher power to achieve the same effect — an arms race that will keep evolving on both sides. And HPM’s effective range, typically 1 to 3 kilometers for current fielded systems, is considerably shorter than a laser’s or a missile’s. [FACT/INFERENCE — range figures for HPM systems are generally not published with laser-level precision by manufacturers for security reasons; the 1–3km figure is a commonly cited open-source approximation, not a disclosed Leonidas specification] 𝗛𝗢𝗪 𝗪𝗘 𝗚𝗢𝗧 𝗛𝗘𝗥𝗘: FROM STAR WARS TO THE STRYKER Directed energy is not a new idea; it is an old idea that finally has the power electronics, the beam control, and the target set to justify the expense. The concept traces back at least to the 1980s Strategic Defense Initiative — the program the press nicknamed “Star Wars” — which chased space-based laser missile defense against Soviet ICBMs and, for all the ridicule it drew at the time, seeded much of the beam-control and adaptive-optics research that today’s fielded systems now depend on. [FACT] Early practical shipboard demonstrators, like the Navy’s LaWS (Laser Weapon System) in the 2010s, were genuine technology firsts but remained experimental, single-ship curiosities rather than combat-integrated weapons. What changed between LaWS and HELIOS was not the underlying physics — it was the proliferation of cheap drones and loitering munitions that finally created a target set numerous and persistent enough to justify the investment, combined with a decade of progress in solid-state fiber lasers and gallium-nitride microwave amplifiers that made both power output and reliability finally viable in a warship or ground-vehicle-sized package. 𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝒂𝒍𝒍𝒆𝒈𝒆𝒅 '𝑶𝒍𝒐𝒅𝒐 𝒖𝒑𝒓𝒊𝒔𝒊𝒏𝒈,’ 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝑫𝒖𝒏𝒏𝒊𝒏𝒈-𝑲𝒓𝒖𝒈𝒆𝒓 𝑬𝒇𝒇𝒆𝒄𝒕, 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝑫𝒆𝒍𝒖𝒔𝒊𝒐𝒏 𝒐𝒇 𝑺𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕𝒏𝒆𝒔𝒔.   Discussions about "the olodo uprising" became a trend in Nigeria a few weeks ago. I saw a few posts on X and Threads about it, and after laughing at the humour of the phrase, I asked myself whether it was really an uprising or whether people are just getting tired of the brazenness and boldness of the ignoramus. Then I saw a post that said something along the lines of "The Dunning-Kruger effect and the olodo uprising." And I thought, oh oh, this is getting a bit 'sciencey' now. Olodo is a Yoruba expression. A noun that literally translates to “the owner of zeros.” It is a not-too-playful way of describing someone who often scores 0% on any classwork. If you are being called an olodo, it either means you are generally dumb or specifically ignorant of the particular topic being discussed. Being ignorant, I meant being an olodo is not necessarily a disease, but being ignorant of your ignorance or being arrogant despite, and/or about your ignorance is where many non-olodos draw the line. Before we go far, it's worth noting that the purported uprising or invasion by Olodos and brainrot culture is not peculiar to Nigeria, nor to this generation. It will only take you a few online video flips to come across American and British youths who do not know the countries or water bodies bordering them to the north or south, or who think Africa or Europe is a single country. Ignorance in one place does not justify ignorance in another place, but it would be unfair to nationalise the human condition to Nigeria or to make it a generational issue. Of course, there is suspicion that for the first time in human history, a generation might actually be worse than its predecessor - but that's a topic for another day. Let's leave the olodos for a moment and talk about knowledge. Is Knowledge overrated? This is a question I have asked myself so often – because being athletic, funny, a good liar, and being ready to flaunt your body (especially if it is a fine one) or make a fool of yourself will earn you far more than being knowledgeable. Also, being knowledgeable often makes you seem like the odd one among friends, family and peers. People have different approaches to and views on knowledge. Some search and hunger for knowledge, while some ignore it and revel in their lack of it. Knowledge is a burden, and ignorance is bliss. Each to his or her own poison, as always. As I have mentioned above, there is nothing wrong with not knowing. But then it's one thing not to know, and another to know that you do not know. The problem lies with people who are ignorant of their ignorance, thus becoming a cocky simpleton -- the empty barrels that make the most noise. According to one of the 'oriental maxims', there are four states of knowledge: stupid, simple, asleep, and wise. These arose from the saying:  "He who knows not, and knows not he knows not, is a fool; shun him. He who knows not, and knows he knows not, is simple; teach him. He who knows, and knows not he knows, is asleep; awaken him. He who knows, and knows he knows, is wise; follow him."  I heard this from Neil deGrasse Tyson, by the way, but the saying is often credited to Bruce Lee, and, like most sayings, I am not sure it is being credited correctly. There is a consensus that it can be traced back to a Socratic paradox, “I know that I know nothing.” Also put as, “True understanding stems from recognising the limits of your own understanding.” This was put differently by John Fenwick, who said, "The more a man knows, the more he knows that he knows not." While Joseph Campbell put it as, "He who thinks he knows, doesn't know. He who knows that he doesn't know, knows." Put simply, being that there are too many things out there to know, the first step to knowing anything is knowing that you know nothing. As those who hold on to the understanding that they do not know are likely to open their minds, search for, and gain knowledge, while those who are ignorant of their ignorance will continue to glory in their ignorance and argue as if they know all. Hence, another of my favourite sayings attributed to the 13th-century poet Rumi, "You can silence 50 scholars with one fact, but you cannot silence one idiot with 50 facts." There are other wise men between Rumi and the current days who have said similar things. Charles Darwin said, "Ignorance more frequently begets confidence than does knowledge." While Bertrand Russell succinctly put it as, "In the modern world, the stupid are cocksure while the intelligent are full of doubt." This brings me to the Dunning-Kruger effect. The Dunning-Kruger effect is a cognitive bias in which individuals with low ability in a particular domain overestimate their skills and competence, while highly skilled individuals may underestimate theirs. i.e. people who do not know think they are experts, and the experts think they (the experts) do not know enough. It is said to be due to a metacognitive deficit, in which individuals do not know enough about a topic to know that they are ignorant. There is a funny story behind the 1999 study by the two psychologists (Dunning and Kruger), but no point in prolonging an already long post. It is obvious from the definition of the Dunning-Kruger effect that it is more all-encompassing - skills, ability and competence. Not just about knowledge. Even without going sciencey, people generally tend to overestimate their talents. I am sure most of us have seen one or two singers on talent shows who came in believing they were the best in the world (and their mums or dads or grannies told them so too), only for them to sing and become everlasting memes and butts of international jokes. This tendency can be used in explaining why Jamie from the junkyard is arguing with an astrophysicist about the shape of the planet, or why a mediocre footballer might claim he is a better striker than Osimhen, and that it is just a lack of luck and connections that has restricted him to playing football on the beach all his life. Or why a semi-talented singer will believe she sings better than Tiwa Savage, or why a non-funny comedian will claim he is funnier than Basketmouth... I hope you get the gist. Very few people are objective enough about how talented or talentless they are. There are many critiques and seminar discussions on the Dunning-Kruger effect; you can Google it if you are one of those greedy people who want to know everything. Finally, all these bring me to what I call the delusion of smartness. Smartness is a vague characterisation, because the word is often used to describe many unrelated attributes. But the common thing that comes to mind when someone tells you that you are either smart or not smart is a judgment of your IQ level. Now, IQ tests measure your verbal comprehension, working memory, information processing speed, and perceptual/spatial reasoning. Which means that just because you have a high IQ does not mean you are knowledgeable, because being knowledgeable is a case of how much information of different kinds you have accumulated and, of course, are able to remember. Also, having a high IQ score and/or being knowledgeable does not mean you are wise, because wisdom is more about the ability to reason logically, learn from experience, and use acquired knowledge to make sound decisions and navigate life's complexities. As another saying goes, judging a fish by its ability to climb trees will make the fish spend its entire life thinking it is untalented, just as judging a monkey by its ability to swim. Thus, intelligence does not end with IQ (book smart); there are other areas of intelligence, such as emotional intelligence, motor intelligence (athleticism, flexibility, reflexes, and muscle control), creative intelligence (artistry, writing, poetry, etc), and musical intelligence. This means many are talented, but in various ways. However, inasmuch as we should not judge a fish by its ability to climb trees, nor a monkey by its ability to swim, the fish, too, should stay in its lane and argue and advise only on swimming-related issues, and the monkey should create only tree-climbing-related podcasts. Because it is important to bear in mind that; just because you are smart at playing football does not mean you will be smart at talking; just because you are a mathematician or a scientist does not mean that you will be good with political or philosophical discussions; and just because you are skilled in oration (which can make you a good – pastor, imam or leader) does not mean you are wise. Different areas of the brain are responsible for these skills, and unfortunately, very few people are lucky enough to be talented in multiple areas. Unfortunately, again, not all skills can be acquired through learning. However, since most people overestimate their skills, it means most ignorant people do not know enough to know that they do not know, and most dumb people are not smart enough to know that they are dumb – all these might lead to you meeting a lot of people who are going about their days, acting and arguing with the cockiness of an expert despite not knowing a thing and not being skilled at all regarding the topic being discussed. Everyone else around them can see the emptiness. But they will hold firmly to their belief in being smart despite all the evidence pointing to the contrary. Hence why I call it a delusion: a false belief held onto firmly in the face of immutable evidence to the contrary. In the rare instance that you do not know any of those names I referenced, Google them.  Michael Olukayode is a psychiatrist and an author.
